title: Making a cotton rag pulp
subtitle: Preparing worn cotton fabric as papermaking pulp by cooking, beating, and diluting
excerpt: Cotton rags were the primary raw material for Western paper until wood pulp displaced them in the 19th century. Worn cotton clothing, pillowcases, or sheeting can still be converted into papermaking pulp at home. The process has three stages: cutting and cooking to soften the fibres, beating to separate and hydrate them, and diluting for the vat.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1947), chapter on rag preparation and beating. Hunter documents the washing, cutting, and cooking stages for cotton rag papermaking. Internet Archive. The Rittenhouse process and similar 18th-century American rag preparation methods are recorded in early American trade records cited by Hunter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Worn or cut pieces of cotton fabric used as raw material for papermaking. Cotton rag produces strong, acid-free paper with a similar character to cotton linter but with longer fibres and a slightly rougher surface texture.

The mechanical action of breaking down and hydrating cellulose fibres to prepare them for sheet formation. Longer beating produces denser, more translucent sheets; shorter beating produces open, textured sheets.
Sodium carbonate, used in a dilute solution to cook cotton rags and soften their fibres before beating. It also raises the pH, which reduces future acid degradation of the finished paper.

content.text: cook to soften the fibres, a

stage to separate and hydrate them, and a dilution stage for the vat. Only 100% cotton or linen fabric should be used: synthetic fibres do not break down into papermaking-grade cellulose and will produce an unusable pulp. Remove buttons, zips, and any non-cellulose components before starting.

items.name: Worn 100% cotton fabric (old T-shirts, sheets, pillowcases), all non-cotton components removed
Soda ash (sodium carbonate, available from craft dye suppliers)
items.qty: 20 g per litre of cooking water

items.name: Large stainless steel or enamel pot (not aluminium)
Rubber gloves (for handling the hot cooked rags)

attrs.heading: Soda ash safety
attrs.body: Soda ash solution is alkaline and irritates skin and eyes. Wear rubber gloves and work in a ventilated space.

content.text: Wash the fabric thoroughly and cut into pieces no larger than 5 cm square. Smaller pieces cook more evenly and blend more easily.

Dissolve 20 g soda ash per litre of water in the cooking pot. Add the cut fabric and bring to a gentle simmer. Simmer for 2 hours, checking periodically. The water will darken as sizing and dye are released from the fabric. Add more water if the level drops below the fabric.
Drain the cooked fabric in a colander and rinse thoroughly under cold water until the rinse water runs clear. The fabric should now feel soft and slightly fluffy at its cut edges.

In a kitchen blender: add a small piece of wet cooked rag (about 5 g) and fill the blender to two-thirds with clean water. Blend for 30 to 45 seconds. The result should be a smooth, cloudy slurry with no visible fabric chunks. If chunks remain, blend for another 15 seconds.

Repeat in batches until all the cooked fabric is beaten. Combine all batches in the papermaking vat and dilute with water to the standard working concentration. Cotton rag pulp can be formed, couched, pressed, and dried using the same method as cotton linter.

attrs.heading: Common problems with rag pulp
attrs.intro: Problems that appear during cooking and beating.

items.symptom: Fabric still has a stiff, cloth-like texture after cooking
items.cause: Cooking time too short, or soda ash concentration too low.
items.fix: Cook for a further hour. Check that the soda ash was fully dissolved before adding the fabric.

items.symptom: Blended pulp has a grey or brown tint
items.cause: Coloured or patterned fabric releasing dye. White or undyed cotton gives the cleanest result.
items.fix: Use only white or pale-coloured cotton fabric. The colour will fade significantly with thorough rinsing.
